blurb: Helsing is a European defence-AI company building software and increasingly hardware (autonomous aircraft, strike drones, robotics) for military applications, with a strong 'European sovereignty' framing.

controversies.title: HX-2 loitering-munition setbacks in Ukraine — alleged takeoff failures, missing AI capabilities and EW vulnerability; Ukraine paused further orders (company disputes)
controversies.summary: In January 2026 Bloomberg reported (citing anonymous Ukrainian and German sources, and a presentation by a German military unit under Gen. Günter Schneider) that Ukraine held off on new orders of Helsing's HX-2 loitering munition after battlefield setbacks: alleged takeoff failures during trials by the 14th Separate Regiment of the Unmanned Systems Forces, AI-driven flight-control features that reportedly did not work as advertised, and HX-2 units compromised by Russian electronic-warfare (EW) systems causing loss of data link and control. The report followed an earlier April 2025 Bloomberg piece in which soldiers alleged overpriced drones and glitchy software. Helsing issued a rebuttal disputing the report as misleading, said it had active requests from six Ukrainian units and that the trial unit had requested ~1,000 more units, and stood by the HX-2's battlefield testing. UK relevance: Helsing has a London office (and an open London Legal Counsel role); the episode bears directly on lethal-autonomous-weapons (LAWS) effectiveness/accountability and defence-procurement-assurance debates that UK/European AI-in-defence policy is grappling with. Reported as alleged/disputed — no regulatory finding.
controversies.rawExcerpt: On January 19, 2026, Bloomberg published a report stating that further orders of these UAVs for the Ukrainian military had been suspended due to critical flaws ... Helsing issued a rebuttal the following day, stating that the Bloomberg report was misleading.

news.title: Helsing Introduces the CA-1 Electronic Attack, an Autonomous Combat Aircraft for Electronic Warfare
news.summary: Helsing (Munich) unveils the CA-1, an autonomous combat aircraft for electronic warfare — a notable escalation from defence software into autonomous lethal hardware, directly relevant to lethal-autonomous-weapons (LAWS) policy debates.

news.title: Helsing and EURENCO sign partnership to deliver a sovereign European strike drone capability
news.summary: Partnership with EURENCO to build a 'sovereign European' strike-drone capability — ties autonomous-weapons development to European strategic-autonomy / defence-sovereignty policy.

notes: Harvested 2026-06-27 via OpenClaw headless browser (helsing.ai/newsroom passed cleanly; untested by web_fetch this morning). Dates confirmed from each article's own 'Press Release DD. Mon. YYYY' header. Selected the two items with the strongest AI-policy signal (autonomous combat aircraft; sovereign European strike drones) as defence-AI / LAWS / European-sovereignty signals. Other dated newsroom items exist (OHB/Helsing space JVs 'KIRK'/'Alliance for Europe's Space Sector', RX-1 robotics platform, Stockholm office, CCO/CFO appointments) but are corporate/personnel rather than policy — left as leads, not logged, to keep signal high. Newsroom listing links wrapped images with no inline text on re-query, so dates were read from individual article headers rather than the index. ROLES (2026-06-27 ATS): Legal Counsel (London) open at the defence-AI firm. CONTROVERSY (2026-06-27 20:08 UTC): logged the HX-2 Ukraine-procurement setbacks (Bloomberg 19 Jan 2026 + Apr 2025 allegations; Helsing rebuttal). Treated Defense Express as an untrusted lead and traced its citation back to the dated Bloomberg reports + company response; status=disputed, severity=medium (alleged, no regulatory finding). LAWS/defence-procurement-assurance signal with a London nexus.
